points: A verifier-bound, fail-closed admission predicate that deterministically pins policy hash, public randomness, transcript chaining, canonicalization, latent-schema constraints, and proof/attestation checks reduces covert signaling measurably beyond audit-only or post-hoc detection controls because only verifier-accepted envelopes can advance agent state.

In strict enforcement lanes, covert recoverability was empirically suppressed to a maximum decoder advantage of 0.0000 with a mutual-information proxy of 0.0636 bits while maintaining minimum utility of 0.8860, indicating that near-zero detectable signaling can coexist with usable agent performance when acceptance semantics are tightly bound.

Operational feasibility is dominated by proving cost rather than verification cost: full-proof mode showed 27.53 s median turn latency (p95 28.08 s) whereas sampled proving reduced non-proved-turn latency to 0.327 ms, enabling a deployable tiering strategy where online verification is always-on and proving cadence is tuned to assurance needs.

nodes.title: Human-Certified Module Repositories for the AI Age
nodes.one_liner: The key move is shifting trust from AI-generated glue code to a curated, attestable inventory of reusable modules with enforceable contracts and audited provenance.
points: Human-Certified Module Repositories (HCMRs) propose a high-governance, provenance-rich, human-reviewed module ecosystem that constrains both developers and AI agents to assemble software only from certified building blocks with explicit interface contracts.

Recent supply-chain failures illustrate the blast radius HCMRs target—SolarWinds propagated signed malicious updates to ~18,000 organizations and the XZ incident impacted a library depended on by nearly 30,000 Debian/Ubuntu packages—implying repository-level trust controls can materially reduce systemic risk.

Security-aware AI coding remains a bottleneck, as one cited benchmark reports ~61% functional task success but only 10.5% of outputs meeting both functional and security requirements, motivating contract- and provenance-enforced composition rather than post-hoc patching.

nodes.title: The Mirror Design Pattern: Strict Data Geometry over Model Scale for Prompt Injection Detection
nodes.one_liner: Careful corpus geometry (matched positive/negative cells) makes a tiny compiled linear model outperform a 22M transformer for first-line prompt-injection screening while being orders of magnitude faster and less promptable.
points: A strictly cell-matched multilingual corpus (31/32 reason×language cells, 5,000 samples) enables a sparse character n-gram linear SVM to reach 95.97% recall and 92.07% F1 on a 524-case holdout at sub-millisecond latency with weights compiled into a static Rust binary (no external model runtime).

On the same holdout, a 22M-parameter Prompt Guard 2 semantic classifier achieves 44.35% recall and 59.14% F1 with 49ms median and 324ms p95 latency, implying that hot-path L1 screening can favor deterministic sparse detectors over small transformers when data is geometrically curated.

Residual risk concentrates in contextual ambiguity rather than mechanics: the linear screen shows a 51.9% false-positive rate on a hard-benign set that quotes/discusses attacks (12.3% at a higher threshold), and both the SVM and Prompt Guard 2 plateau around ~21% recall on heavily paraphrased attacks, motivating a layered architecture where later stages handle use-versus-mention and semantic thinness.

nodes.title: IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs
nodes.one_liner: A programmatically-graded, adversarially-generated RL dataset can significantly harden frontier LLMs’ instruction hierarchy and simultaneously reduce unsafe behavior and prompt-injection success with minimal capability regression.
points: Reinforcement-learning fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on the released IH-Challenge dataset with online adversarial conflict synthesis raises average instruction-hierarchy robustness across 16 benchmarks from 84.1% to 94.1% (+10.0%).

When a safety specification is placed in the system message, the trained model cuts unsafe behavior from 6.6% to 0.7% while largely preserving helpfulness on production-style safety evaluations, indicating IH robustness materially improves safety steerability rather than just refusal rates.

Instruction-hierarchy training also improves prompt-injection robustness—reaching 1.00 on an internal static agentic prompt-injection evaluation (up from 0.44, +0.56) and modestly improving CyberSecEval 2 (0.88→0.91)—but common system-level mitigations show diminishing or negative returns once the base model is robust.

nodes.title: Jailbreak Scaling Laws for Large Language Models: Polynomial-Exponential Crossover
affiliations: Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: Prompt injection doesn’t just raise jailbreak success—it can flip best-of-k scaling from polynomial to exponential, making inference-time sampling a first-class security risk.
points: Adversarial prompt injection changes best-of-k jailbreaking from the previously observed slow polynomial improvement in success with more samples to a much faster exponential regime, implying that simply increasing inference-time sampling can rapidly collapse safety margins under strong injection.

A spin-glass generative model predicts a weak-injection regime where the jailbreak failure probability decays as a power law in k (1−ASR ≍ k^{-ν}) and a strong-injection regime where failure decays exponentially in k (log(1−ASR) scales as −k·const), attributing the crossover to an ordered phase induced by a strong “misalignment field.”

Empirically, longer injected jailbreak suffixes increase both the fitted exponential term (μ) and the polynomial exponent (ν) in log(−log(ASR)) ≈ −ν log k − μ k + const across Llama-family models, indicating that stronger injections simultaneously raise adversarial alignment and reduce effective ‘reasoning depth’ while boosting multi-sample attack success.

nodes.title: Governance Architecture for Autonomous Agent Systems: Threats, Framework, and Engineering Practice
nodes.one_liner: Execution safety for autonomous agents hinges less on content filters and more on tool-call governance, where LLM judges + defense-in-depth layers can deliver high interception with manageable latency but still struggle on supply-chain-style plugins.
points: A four-layer governance stack for LLM agents—execution sandboxing (L1), intent-verification judging (L2), zero-trust inter-agent authorization (L3), and immutable audit logging (L4)—targets execution-layer failures (prompt injection, RAG poisoning, malicious plugins) that dialogue/content guardrails do not reliably cover.

On a 1,081-sample bilingual tool-call benchmark, LLM-based intent judges blocked 93.0–98.5% of TC1/TC2 malicious tool calls while zero-shot NLI baselines stayed below 10% interception, and a two-stage cascade (Qwen3.5-9B→GPT-4o-mini) achieved 91.9–92.6% interception at 1.9–6.7% false-positive rate (local-only cascade: 94.7–95.6% interception at 6.0–9.7% FPR).

Malicious skill plugins (TC3) were materially harder for intent-only checks (75–94% interception with meaningful precision/recall) and the end-to-end 4-layer pipeline reached 96% interception with ~980 ms P50 latency where non-judge layers contributed only ~18 ms, implying practical deployments should rely on layered enforcement rather than L2 alone.

nodes.title: Security Considerations for Artificial Intelligence Agents
nodes.one_liner: The key security shift is that agents turn untrusted content into executable decisions at machine speed, so only hard policy enforcement can reliably bound worst-case outcomes.
points: AI agents materially expand classic injection risk because untrusted text (and multimodal content) can directly steer tool-calling and workflow control, making indirect prompt injection a first-order threat to confidentiality (data exfiltration), integrity (unauthorized actions), and availability (resource exhaustion).

Multi-agent architectures add unique failure modes—implicit delegation, shared-state contamination, and confused-deputy/privilege-escalation via agent chains—making authorization, attribution, and recovery substantially harder than in single-agent systems.

The most reliable current control is defense-in-depth with a deterministic enforcement layer (e.g., tool allow/block lists, rate limits, schema/regex validation, and human confirmation) because input/model-level mitigations remain probabilistic and are undermined in production by base-rate false positives and latency/cost in long-running workflows.

nodes.title: Compatibility at a Cost: Systematic Discovery and Exploitation of MCP Clause-Compliance Vulnerabilities
nodes.one_liner: MCP interoperability is effectively buying ecosystem growth with a large, spec-sanctioned attack surface created by optional clause compliance gaps across SDKs.
points: Only 21.5% of MCP’s 275 specification clauses are unconditional MUSTs while 78.5% are optional/conditional, and this “compatibility-first” skew systematically creates enforcement gaps that can be abused as security vulnerabilities rather than mere implementation bugs.

Across 10 official MCP SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go, Kotlin, Swift, Java, C#, Ruby, Rust, PHP), clause-level analysis flagged 1,270 non-implementations and 1,265 potentially exploitable risks, indicating that most missing or relaxed behaviors translate into attacker-relevant control over message payload and/or timing.

A cross-language IR + static-slicing + LLM semantic check achieved ~86% precision and ~87% recall (14% FP, 13.5% FN) at $541.87 total cost for 2,750 clause checks (~$0.20/check), and maintainers acknowledged 20/26 submitted reports with 5 triaged high-priority and invited integration into MCP conformance testing.

nodes.title: Taming OpenClaw: Security Analysis and Mitigation of Autonomous LLM Agent Threats
nodes.one_liner: Agent security fails when defenses are scoped to a single step—autonomy turns small prompt/tool weaknesses into cross-session, cross-stage compromise unless the entire lifecycle is mediated end-to-end.
points: A five-stage lifecycle threat model (initialization, input, inference, decision, execution) shows how agent compromises propagate across time, turning single-entry weaknesses (e.g., indirect prompt injection) into multi-stage system compromise (e.g., unauthorized tool use, data exfiltration, and host impact).

Real-world exposure is materially driven by the tool ecosystem, with prior large-scale audits cited indicating ~26% of community-contributed agent skills contain security vulnerabilities, making supply-chain compromise and credential leakage a first-order risk for autonomous agents.

Point defenses (filters/guardrails at one interface) are insufficient against cross-temporal attacks such as memory poisoning and intent drift, motivating defense-in-depth controls that combine plugin provenance checks, context-aware instruction filtering, memory integrity validation/rollback, intent verification, and capability-enforced sandboxing.

nodes.title: VoiceSHIELD-Small: Real-Time Malicious Speech Detection and Transcription
nodes.one_liner: A lightweight, MIT-licensed joint ASR+audio-safety head on Whisper shows that real-time (<100 ms) malicious speech screening can be integrated without waiting for text transcription—shifting moderation earlier in the audio pipeline.
points: A Whisper-small–encoder + mean-pooling + 0.13M-parameter MLP head achieves 99.16% accuracy and 0.9865 F1 on a 947-sample held-out test set while doing safety classification in 45–120 ms on GPUs (vs. ~250–320 ms for typical ASR→text-moderation pipelines).

At the default decision threshold (τ=0.2), the system attains 99.66% precision, 97.67% recall, 0.15% false-positive rate, and a 2.33% false-negative rate (7/300 malicious clips missed), implying high trust in flags but non-trivial residual risk for high-stakes blocking without layered controls.

Five-fold stratified cross-validation reports tightly clustered performance (F1 = 0.9879 ± 0.0026, ROC-AUC = 0.9989 ± 0.0009), but the evaluation is limited by studio-quality English-only audio and modest scale (6,310 clips), signaling likely degradation under distribution shift (noise, accents, telephony codecs, novel attack patterns).

nodes.title: AttriGuard: Defeating Indirect Prompt Injection in LLM Agents via Causal Attribution of Tool Invocations
nodes.one_liner: Instead of trying to spot malicious text, it tests whether a tool call still happens when external observations are stripped of their control power—and blocks calls that don't survive the counterfactual replay.
points: AttriGuard shifts indirect prompt-injection defense from input semantics to action-level causal attribution and achieves 0% static attack success rate (ASR) across four LLMs and two agent benchmarks while keeping benign utility loss to ~3% with ~2× token overhead.

Against optimization-based adaptive attackers with full defense knowledge and up to 200 queries per instance, AttriGuard holds single-digit ASR (6.56% on Gemini-2.5 and 9.84% on Llama3.3-70B) while leading baselines degrade to 29.5%–82.0% ASR, indicating a substantial robustness gap under adaptation.

Ablations show the security–utility tradeoff is primarily controlled by attenuation strength (λ=1 raises ASR to 12.17% while improving utility), and reliability depends on teacher-forced shadow replay plus fuzzy tool-call survival to reduce false positives from trajectory drift and LLM stochasticity.

nodes.title: OpenClaw PRISM: A Zero-Fork, Defense-in-Depth Runtime Security Layer for Tool-Augmented LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: A practical agent-defense stack can outperform prompt-only guardrails by spreading enforcement across the agent lifecycle, but LLM-assisted scanning is a latency budget you must explicitly manage.
points: On an 80-case unified benchmark slice spanning lifecycle flows and tool-policy scenarios, enabling all layers (hooks + scanner + proxy) increased attack block rate from 0.000 (no protection) to 0.955 while holding false positives at 0.139, indicating most gains came from enforceable tool governance rather than text filtering alone.

Adding scanner-assisted post-tool inspection improved F1 from 0.563 (plugin-only) to 0.658 (plugin+scanner) by raising recall from 0.455 to 0.545 and lowering false positives from 0.194 to 0.139, showing that tool-result scrutiny materially reduces indirect-injection and contextual attack miss rates.

Runtime cost is dominated by LLM-based scanning latency: p95 per-case time jumped from 0.583 ms (plugin-only) to 12,498 ms (plugin+scanner) and 15,775 ms (full stack) in the harness, implying production deployments need aggressive escalation gating, timeouts, and selective scanning to keep agent UX acceptable.

nodes.title: The Struggle Between Continuation and Refusal: A Mechanistic Analysis of the Continuation-Triggered Jailbreak in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: A minimal formatting tweak can flip aligned chat models from refusal to harmful continuation, and the effect localizes to a small set of causally testable attention heads that can be dialed up or down at inference time.
points: A purely structural prompt change—moving a continuation-triggered suffix (e.g., “Sure, here is… First,”) outside the user marker so it is parsed as assistant continuation—boosts Attack Success Rate from 0 to 0.16/0.26/0.58 on AdvBench/JailbreakBench/MaliciousInstruct for LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and up to 0.68 on MaliciousInstruct for Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct.

Causal head-level interventions (path patching + ablation) isolate a small, middle-to-late-layer set of “safety heads” whose removal increases ASR and “continuation heads” whose removal decreases ASR, indicating jailbreaks emerge from a competition between continuation drive and alignment-enforced refusal rather than from uniformly distributed model behavior.

Inference-time activation scaling provides strong behavioral control: amplifying safety heads can cut ASR sharply (e.g., LLaMA-2-7B-Chat on MaliciousInstruct from 0.58 to 0.16 when scaling three safety heads at w=5) while amplifying continuation heads can explosively raise ASR (e.g., from 0.58 to 0.92 when scaling three continuation heads), but the safety mechanism differs by architecture (harmfulness recognition in LLaMA-2 vs refusal execution in Qwen2.5).

nodes.title: WebWeaver: Breaking Topology Confidentiality in LLM Multi-Agent Systems with Stealthy Context-Based Inference
nodes.one_liner: Topology can be stolen as IP from LLM multi-agent systems without asking for IDs—agent “voiceprints” in routine dialogue are enough to recover the graph, even under simple content filters.
points: Compromising just one non-admin agent is sufficient to reconstruct a full LLM multi-agent communication topology by fingerprinting agents from dialogue context (sender predictor F1: 0.877 on CSQA, 0.947 on GSM8K, 0.960 on Fact, 0.983 on Bias).

Under active keyword-based defenses that defeat ID-query attacks, context-only topology inference remains effective and the full WebWeaver pipeline reports ~60% higher inference accuracy than prior baselines while maintaining negligible overhead.

A jailbreak-free fallback can complete the topology via masked diffusion (DDPM) while provably preserving known edges during sampling, reaching MRR up to 0.866 (Bias) and F1 ≈0.789–0.820 across tasks even when jailbreaks fail.

nodes.title: ADVERSA: Measuring Multi-Turn Guardrail Degradation and Judge Reliability in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Multi-turn safety evaluation changes when you measure per-round compliance trajectories and explicitly quantify both attacker reliability (refusals/drift) and judge disagreement instead of treating jailbreaks as binary events.
points: Across 15 multi-turn red-teaming conversations (≤10 rounds) against three frontier models, 26.7% (4/15) met the jailbreak threshold, with 75% of jailbreaks (3/4) occurring in round 1 and an average jailbreak round of 1.25, indicating failures were dominated by first-turn framing rather than gradual erosion under sustained pressure in this setting.

A fine-tuned 70B attacker (QLoRA) was necessary because an off-the-shelf attacker model refused ~85% of attack-generation requests, but attacker-side failures still measurably confounded results—3 attacker refusals occurred and all were against one victim model (Gemini 3.1 Pro), artificially reducing its effective attack exposure and inflating apparent resistance.

Using a triple-judge (Claude/Gemini/GPT) consensus made evaluator uncertainty visible: all jailbreak decisions were unanimous (high precision on clear harmful compliance), while disagreements concentrated at the 1↔2 boundary (hard vs soft refusal), showing that single-judge scoring can swing outcomes on borderline cases and should be instrumented rather than assumed reliable.

nodes.title: Cascade: Composing Software-Hardware Attack Gadgets for Adversarial Threat Amplification in Compound AI Systems
affiliations: The University of Texas at Austin
nodes.one_liner: Security of compound AI systems is not just about prompts and alignment—legacy CVEs and hardware fault/side-channel primitives can be composed into practical chains that bypass guardrails and tool boundaries.
points: Cross-stack attack chaining can defeat common compound-AI defenses by combining traditional system flaws with LLM weaknesses, including an end-to-end safety break that uses a DoS on the query enhancer plus a Rowhammer-style guardrail fault to pass an unmodified jailbreak prompt to the generator.

In a guardrail evasion study over 50 harmful prompts, targeted token bitflips bypassed the guardrail 82% of the time, targeted attention-mask flips worked 72% of the time, and random attention-mask flips reached 94%—implying guardrails can be highly brittle to small memory corruptions even without changing the downstream model.

Adversarial suffix jailbreaks generated with a GCG-style method achieved an 80% jailbreak success rate (41 prompts) with substantial compute cost (~123 minutes per prompt on a 4×NVIDIA L40S setup), indicating attackers can trade GPU time for reliable safety violations once pipeline controls are bypassed.

nodes.title: Deactivating Refusal Triggers: Understanding and Mitigating Overrefusal in Safety Alignment
nodes.one_liner: Overrefusal is driven less by “being too safe” in the abstract and more by specific benign-looking cues that become refusal-anchored during safety fine-tuning—and you can counteract it by training directly on those cues.
points: Overrefusal arises when safety alignment causes models to internalize “refusal triggers” (non-harmful linguistic cues like generic help-seeking phrasing and benign events) that become statistically associated with refusals, leading to benign queries being rejected when they contain similar cues.

Behavioral and representational evidence indicates rejected benign queries are closer in hidden-state space to extracted refusal triggers than accepted benign queries, and refusal rates fall sharply as triggers are paraphrased to be less similar (e.g., SFT α=1: 100.0% refusal on original triggers vs 96.39% at Level-3; P-SFT α=1: 94.73% vs 61.85%).

Replacing generic benign regularization data (Alpaca) with a small trigger-matched benign set (~248 samples) substantially improves the safety–utility trade-off, cutting refusal on benign benchmarks while retaining low jailbreak success (e.g., Llama3-U SFT Avg 90.04→32.90 and Qwen2.5-U SFT Avg 67.73→31.63 vs Alpaca; RLVR on Llama3-U Avg 45.69→30.22).

nodes.title: Risk-Adjusted Harm Scoring for Automated Red Teaming for LLMs in Financial Services
nodes.one_liner: Severity- and confidence-weighted scoring (RAHS) stays informative when multi-turn red teaming pushes traditional success rates to ~99%, which is exactly the regime financial deployments must plan for.
points: A BFSI-specific benchmark (989 adversarial prompts across 7 risk areas) shows that finance-themed requests framed as legal or professionally plausible can reliably elicit operationally actionable harmful disclosures, exposing a mismatch between generic safety guardrails and regulated-domain risk semantics.

Raising decoding temperature generally increases jailbreak frequency and worsens risk-weighted outcomes (e.g., Qwen2.5-32B ASR 87.4%→90.1% from T=0 to T=1.0 while RAHS drops -0.318→-0.330), indicating that sampling stochasticity can convert borderline behavior into higher-severity, higher-consensus failures.

Adaptive multi-turn attacks rapidly drive models toward near-ceiling compromise rates (e.g., Qwen3-32B ASR 95.9% at round 2 to 99.5% at round 5, domyn-small-9B 94.7%→98.3%) while RAHS continues to separate models even when ASR saturates (R5 RAHS: -0.363 vs -0.266), implying that severity-aware metrics are necessary for meaningful differentiation under sustained adversarial pressure.

nodes.title: Multi-Stream Perturbation Attack: Breaking Safety Alignment of Thinking LLMs Through Concurrent Task Interference
nodes.one_liner: Concurrent-task interference is an unusually effective jailbreak lever against thinking-mode models because it simultaneously hides malicious intent from sequence-based safety checks and destabilizes long-form reasoning into loops or collapse.
points: Interleaving a harmful request with multiple benign “task streams” in a single prompt (plus character-reversal or output-shape constraints) bypasses safety alignment on thinking-mode LLMs, with MS_Reverse reported to exceed 90% attack success rate on some model/dataset settings and to outperform common baselines across JailbreakBench, AdvBench, and HarmBench.

The same perturbations degrade reasoning stability in thinking mode, producing measurable failure modes with thinking-collapse rates up to 17% and response-repetition rates up to 60% (e.g., Qwen3 4B), while most baseline jailbreak methods remain near 0–2% on these stability metrics.

Multi-stream perturbations induce extreme inference overhead in thinking mode—often pushing reasoning traces beyond 10k–20k characters and reaching ~7 minutes per query on an API model—turning jailbreaking into a combined safety and availability/DoS risk rather than a pure content-policy bypass.

nodes.title: Systematic Scaling Analysis of Jailbreak Attacks in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Normalizing jailbreaks by FLOPs reveals predictable saturation curves and shows that fluent prompt-rewriting can beat gradient-based suffix search on both efficiency and stealth.
points: Across four jailbreak paradigms (GCG, PAIR, best-of-n sampling, AutoDAN), success vs. attacker compute (TFLOPs) consistently shows rapid early gains followed by diminishing-returns saturation, and a simple saturating exponential fit achieves high goodness-of-fit (e.g., R² up to 0.997 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct).

Prompt-rewriting (PAIR) is markedly more compute-efficient than gradient-based suffix search (GCG) under the same FLOPs budget, reaching a much higher red-team-score ceiling (9.80 vs. 5.16) with far faster saturation (B95 ≈ 6,012 vs. 13,923 TFLOPs), implying that black-box prompting can dominate white-box optimization in practical attacker-cost models.

Jailbreak risk is highly non-uniform: misinformation goals start easier (higher baseline success) than other harm types, and attack methods occupy distinct success–stealth trade-offs where prompting-based methods cluster in the high-success/high-stealth region, suggesting defenses must prioritize deception/misinformation and evaluate detectability alongside success.

nodes.title: Models as Lego Builders: Assembling Malice from Benign Blocks via Semantic Blueprints
affiliations: NLP & KG Lab, Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Multimodal safety filters can be bypassed by splitting a prohibited request into harmless-looking “semantic Lego blocks” inside a diagram and letting the model recombine them during completion.
points: StructAttack exploits a semantic slot-filling failure mode where LVLMs complete locally benign “slot types” (e.g., “Raw Materials”, “Making Process”) into globally harmful instructions, reaching ~69% attack success on GPT-4o and ~50–74% on Gemini/Qwen3-VL across Advbench-M and SafeBench with a single query.

Structured visual prompts (mind maps/tables/sunburst) materially amplify this vulnerability: a text-only slot-filling template already achieves 54% ASR on a 70-sample GPT-4o subset, and adding visual structure plus small random perturbations raises performance further (e.g., 62.9%→67.1% ASR on GPT-4o).

Compared to prior typographic and OOD jailbreaks, StructAttack is both stronger and cheaper, achieving 65.7% ASR with 1 victim-model query and ~7.1% refusal rate versus SI-Attack (37.1% ASR, 10 queries, 28.6% refusal) and JOOD (38.6% ASR, 45 queries, 27.1% refusal).

nodes.title: Backdoor4Good: Benchmarking Beneficial Uses of Backdoors in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Backdoor-style trigger conditioning can be repurposed into a high-precision, low-overhead control interface for safety, access, personalization, and attribution—yet multi-trigger interference shows governance needs explicit arbitration, not naive stacking.
points: Across Llama3.1-8B, Gemma-2-9B, Qwen2.5-7B, and Llama2-13B, trigger-conditioned behaviors achieve near-perfect controllability (average TAR with trigger ≈ 0.97) while keeping unintended activation very low (average TAR without trigger < 0.02), indicating a large and reliable activation gap often > 0.95.

General capability is largely preserved after injecting beneficial backdoors via a small LoRA fine-tune (4 epochs, lr=2e-4), with TruthfulQA/MT-Bench and GLUE (MNLI/RTE/SST-2) staying close to baseline, implying conditional control can be added without meaningful utility loss.

Beneficial backdoors are data-efficient (often reaching near-max TAR with only 10–20 trigger examples) but not fully compositional, as multi-trigger training can create dominance/suppression effects (e.g., Gemma-2-9B access-control TAR drops to ~0.82 even in single-task settings and degrades further under multi-trigger conflicts).

nodes.title: Synergistic Directed Execution and LLM-Driven Analysis for Zero-Day AI-Generated Malware Detection
nodes.one_liner: Using an LLM as a path oracle for concolic execution can turn path explosion into a manageable search problem while materially boosting zero-day detection on AI-generated malware.
points: LLM-guided concolic exploration reached 95% malicious-code coverage with 1,860±95 explored paths, cutting path search by 73.2% versus DFS (6,950±278) while preserving equivalent malicious-path coverage.

On a 2,500-sample AI-generated malware benchmark, the hybrid system achieved 97.5±0.2% accuracy and 0.993 AUC, exceeding angr-only by +19.3 pp accuracy and outperforming signature/rule baselines by up to +52.2 pp, indicating conventional detectors degrade sharply on LLM-synthesized polymorphism.

Component removals show most of the AI-generated malware lift comes from concolic execution (-15.4 pp accuracy without it) and LLM prioritization (-9.2 pp), with RL policy refinement adding a smaller but consistent gain (-1.7 pp), implying robust deployments should prioritize analysis coverage and guided path selection before online adaptation.

nodes.title: Before You Hand Over the Wheel: Evaluating LLMs for Security Incident Analysis
nodes.one_liner: LLMs can nearly perfect alert triage but still routinely fail to finish full incident investigations, so SOC deployment should start with triage and add strong guardrails for tool-use and dependency cascades.
points: An agentic benchmark for Security Incident Analysis was built with 25 deep-investigation incidents (229 investigative questions) plus 135 alert-triage scenarios (80 false positives, 55 true positives), covering network/memory forensics, malware, phishing, and logs with MITRE ATT&CK tactic labels and difficulty tiers.

Even the best incident-analysis models only fully solved 8/25 (Claude-4.5-Sonnet) and 5/25 (GPT-5) end-to-end scenarios despite high partial accuracy (~81.7% and ~80.7% respectively), showing that multi-step, multi-objective closure remains the main reliability gap for SOC adoption.

Alert triage is comparatively strong for top models (GPT-5: 98% accuracy on 100 TII-SRC-23 alerts and 97.1% on 35 CIC-IDS2017 alerts), implying immediate value in false-positive reduction, while deeper tasks fail primarily via question dependency, wrong commands, and looping (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B hits round-exhaustion 62.9%).

nodes.title: KEPo: Knowledge Evolution Poison on Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation
affiliations: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China
Ubiquitous Intelligence and Trusted Services Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
nodes.one_liner: Poisoning GraphRAG becomes much easier when the attacker forges a believable timeline that makes the false claim look like the 'latest update' rather than an isolated contradiction.
points: KEPo raises GraphRAG poisoning effectiveness from low double-digit CASR under prior methods to as high as 63.5% average CASR in GraphRAG local search (vs 31.5–52.1% for baselines) by forging time-ordered “knowledge evolution” paths that integrate toxic facts into high-ranking KG communities.

GraphRAG’s global-search mode is materially harder to poison than local search (e.g., KEPo average ASR 33.1% global vs 73.1% local), implying community-summary retrieval offers partial resilience but still fails when poisoned facts are embedded inside otherwise relevant communities.

Common RAG defenses barely reduce KEPo impact (token retention ~98.6–99.7% and ASR essentially unchanged), indicating that toxicity/prompt-pattern filters and query paraphrasing do not address graph-level semantic/temporal plausibility attacks.

nodes.title: Targeted Bit-Flip Attacks on LLM-Based Agents
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: A few carefully chosen hardware-induced bit flips can reliably backdoor an LLM agent’s multi-step behavior—down to which external tools it calls—while keeping normal outputs largely unchanged.
points: Flip-Agent achieves high-impact targeted compromise of LLM-based agents with only 50 parameter bit flips, reaching 92.6–99.2% attack success rate (ASR) while preserving 90–100% clean-data accuracy (CDA) on prompt-triggered WebShop shopping tasks across six quantized (8-bit) LLMs.

Agent-specific, multi-stage attack surfaces enable either (i) steering the final answer or (ii) silently redirecting intermediate tool choices; on ToolBench invocation manipulation, Flip-Agent reaches 98.9–100% ASR with 95–98% CDA on multiple models, demonstrating covert control of tool routing without breaking task outputs.

Existing targeted bit-flip baselines transfer poorly to agents (often <30% ASR on internal-trigger settings), and even an idealized 'protect the critical bits' defense leaves ASR >90% after blocking 50–100 identified bits, implying that bit-level hardening must be combined with broader fault tolerance and integrity checks rather than relying on protecting a small parameter subset.

nodes.title: CLIOPATRA: Extracting Private Information from LLM Insights
nodes.one_liner: Layered heuristic privacy (redaction + clustering + summarization + LLM audit) is brittle to targeted poisoning, whereas differential privacy is the only mitigation that consistently shifts leakage back toward baseline.
points: A targeted poisoning-and-prompt-injection pipeline can extract a user’s sensitive medical condition from “privacy-preserving” LLM insight summaries in 39% of cases with only age, gender, and one symptom (vs. a 22% baseline guesser).

Attack success climbs to 81% on a Qwen-configured pipeline with the same minimal side information and approaches ~100% when the attacker knows five symptoms, showing that modest additional auxiliary knowledge can collapse heuristic privacy guarantees.

LLM-based privacy auditing fails catastrophically—detecting 0 of 205 successful disease extractions (often rating leaked summaries 5/5 privacy)—while DP-based analytics substantially reduce leakage toward baseline at ε=25 but degrade at ε=50, implying formal privacy is necessary yet operationally hard to tune.

nodes.title: Where Do LLM-based Systems Break? A System-Level Security Framework for Risk Assessment and Treatment
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that system-level path modeling (not model-only analysis) makes it obvious where a small number of controls break many multi-step LLM attack chains—and how much risk reduction you get per unit cost.
points: A goal-driven Attack–Defense Tree (ADT) workflow unifies conventional cyber, adversarial-ML, and conversational attacks into end-to-end paths and shows that many heterogeneous threats collapse onto a small set of dominant paths and shared chokepoints (e.g., prompt channel, session/cache boundaries, orchestrator integrity).

Across the healthcare case study, most high-priority branches for clinical integrity (G1), EHR confidentiality (G2), and availability (G3) cluster at CVSS v3.1 base 7.5 (High) with path exploitability Epath = 3.89, implying that Internet-reachable, low-complexity, no-privilege attack surfaces can yield consistently high systemic risk even when the attack family differs.

Cost-aware defense portfolios can materially reduce exploitability: for prompt injection (G1-B1), hardening all preconditions drops Epath from 3.89 to 1.62 and the base score from 7.5 (High) to 5.3 (Medium), while “execution-only” guardrails achieve a smaller reduction to Epath = 2.22 and base 5.9 (Medium), highlighting diminishing returns once the SAND bottleneck shifts.

nodes.title: Evaluating Generalization Mechanisms in Autonomous Cyber Attack Agents
affiliations: University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP)
nodes.one_liner: Even “cosmetic” IP renaming can break long-horizon cyber attack policies, and today’s strongest robustness comes from reasoning-centric LLM agents rather than RL value functions—at a steep cost in reliability and compute.
points: A minimal distribution shift—reassigning host/subnet IPs while keeping enterprise structure fixed—causes severe generalization failures in identifier-dependent agents, including a DDQN+embedding attacker that drops from 100% training win rate to 0% on the unseen reassignment.

Among learning-based approaches, explicit address-invariant abstraction transfers best, with Conceptual Q-learning achieving 65.53%±4.77 win rate on the held-out IP reassignment versus MAML’s 40.00%±0.86 and Reptile’s 2.76%±1.47 (near random), showing that test-time adaptation alone is insufficient without stronger invariances.

Prompt-driven LLM agents generalize most effectively under IP reassignment (ReAct at 95.1% win rate), but failures are dominated by operational brittleness—92.3% of ReAct losses come from invalid-action exhaustion and LLM-BERT’s unseen win rate (51.6%) coincides with slightly negative mean return (-6.5), indicating costly loops despite moderate success.

nodes.title: Compartmentalization-Aware Automated Program Repair
affiliations: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: Making trust boundaries explicit and driving LLM patching with fuzzer feedback turns “LLM-as-a-coder” into “LLM-as-a-compartment-interface hardener,” sharply improving where fixes land and whether they respect the threat model.
points: General-purpose/code-centric LLMs are unreliable for repairing Compartment Interface Vulnerabilities (CIVs) because they lack compartmentalization context (trust boundaries, attacker model, and interface semantics), leading to misplaced or incomplete fixes.

A compartmentalization-aware APR loop that combines CIV fuzzing, CIV/type + call-stack analysis for patch-site selection, and iterative fuzz-based validation can consistently place fixes on the trusted side of the interface and avoid invalidating the sandbox threat model.

In the Apache HTTPD + sandboxed mod_markdown case study (10 runs), naive prompting achieved only 10% (GPT-4o-mini) and 20% (GPT-5) correct patch placement while GPT-5 broke the trust model 50% of the time, whereas the compartmentalization-aware framework achieved 100% correct placement and 0% trust-model breaks.

nodes.title: AgenticCyOps: Securing Multi-Agentic AI Integration in Enterprise Cyber Operations
nodes.one_liner: The most striking result is that simply restructuring agent-to-tool and agent-to-memory integration as explicit trust boundaries cuts the attack surface by ~72% while still supporting end-to-end SOC workflows.
points: Enterprise multi-agent LLM security risks collapse into two dominant trust boundaries—tool orchestration and memory management—despite spanning component-, coordination-, and protocol-layer attacks (e.g., unauthorized access, confused deputy, and memory poisoning).

A phase-scoped, Host-mediated SOC architecture with consensus validation loops can intercept 3 of 4 representative multi-step attack chains within the first two steps, indicating defense-in-depth is achievable before irreversible actions execute.

Applying phase-based capability scoping plus mediated memory access reduced exploitable trust boundaries by at least 72% versus a flat multi-agent baseline (200→56), turning broad implicit trust into a smaller set of explicitly verified checkpoints.

nodes.title: DistillGuard: Evaluating Defenses Against LLM Knowledge Distillation
nodes.one_liner: Output-level API defenses rarely stop knowledge distillation unless they also significantly degrade the service, and even then the protection is highly task-specific.
points: Across 27 defense×benchmark evaluations, only chain-of-thought (CoT) removal meaningfully reduced distillation on math, dropping the distilled student’s MATH-500 accuracy from 67.8% to 31.4% (DE=0.463), while most other defense results stayed near DE≈1.0 (range 0.930–1.026).

Semantic-preserving output perturbation via paraphrasing failed to protect against distillation and sometimes improved the student (e.g., MATH-500 rose to 69.4% at α=1.0; aggregate DE up to 1.012), implying that “useful-to-users” rephrasings largely preserve the distillation signal.

Defenses exhibited sharp task-dependence and unfavorable utility trade-offs: poisoning mostly reduced conversational quality (MT-Bench 8.20→7.63 at 30% poison; DE=0.930) and CoT removal incurred high user harm (overall DC=0.311, with teacher MATH-500 collapsing 78.4%→12.6%), leaving no configuration that achieved both low DE and low DC.

nodes.title: TOSSS: a CVE-based Software Security Benchmark for Large Language Models
affiliations: Ecole Supérieure d’Ingénieurs Léonard de Vinci
nodes.one_liner: A simple A/B choice task built from real CVE fixes reveals large security capability gaps between coding LLMs and shows that a single security hint can measurably shift (and sometimes harm) secure-code preferences.
points: A CVE-grounded two-option code-selection benchmark (secure vs. vulnerable function versions) yields directly interpretable security scores in [0,1] and avoids static-analyzer coverage limits by using real pre-/post-fix commits mined from CVEs.

Across 14 widely used open- and closed-source models evaluated on 1,000 total samples (500 C/C++ + 500 Java), hintless security scores vary substantially from 0.48 (near-random) to 0.89 (consistently secure), indicating meaningful model-to-model differences in vulnerability awareness.

Explicitly instructing models to choose the “most secure” implementation increases average scores by +0.021 (C/C++) and +0.029 (Java), with the largest gains around +0.068 on weaker models but even causing regressions for one coding-specialized model (Codestral 2508: −0.072 C/C++, −0.066 Java).

nodes.title: FlexServe: A Fast and Secure LLM Serving System for Mobile Devices with Flexible Resource Isolation
nodes.one_liner: Flexible, page-level isolation plus secure accelerator switching turns TrustZone from a performance blocker into a near-native on-device LLM serving substrate—even under multi-model, memory-pressured mobile workloads.
points: FlexServe replaces TrustZone’s contiguous secure-memory requirement with page-granular Flexible Secure Memory (Flex-Mem), cutting 8GB secure allocation latency from 6.44s (CMA) to 0.569s (~11.33×) and 8GB reclaim from 733ms to 80.5ms (~12.6×) to remove TTFT bottlenecks under memory fragmentation and pressure.

By adding Flexible Secure NPU (Flex-NPU) that time-multiplexes the NPU into a protected mode (0.21ms mode switch) while restricting DMA via SMMU mappings, FlexServe achieves near-normal-world performance with only ~4.41% average TTFT overhead versus the unprotected baseline and ~2.46% average SPEC CPU virtualization overhead (eliminable via on-demand protection).

End-to-end responsiveness improves sharply in secure on-device LLM serving: FlexServe delivers 10.05× average TTFT speedup over a TrustZone strawman (CPU-only) and 2.44× over an optimized TrustZone baseline (pipeline + secure NPU), while multi-model agent workflows see up to 24.30× and 4.05× speedups respectively—highlighting that flexible isolation is decisive for real mobile multi-model workloads.

nodes.title: MCP-in-SoS: Risk assessment framework for open-source MCP servers
nodes.one_liner: A metadata-grounded CWE→CAPEC scoring pipeline reveals that most open-source MCP servers carry compositional weaknesses where protocol-layer auth gaps frequently enable downstream tool/resource exploitation.
points: Static analysis of 222 open-source Python MCP server repositories found 191 (86.0%) with at least one CWE-mapped weakness, indicating that insecure-by-default implementations are common in the current MCP ecosystem.

Across 15,962 findings spanning 51 CWE classes, the top five weaknesses (CWE-862, CWE-200, CWE-306, CWE-287, CWE-89) account for 75.7% of all findings and appear in 93.2% of affected repositories, showing strong systemic concentration in missing/weak authz/authn and data exposure plus frequent injection risk.

Risk banding places 47.6% of repositories in High risk and 18.3% in Very High, and conditional co-occurrence shows multi-stage exploitability where Tool weaknesses coincide with Protocol weaknesses 87% of the time and Prompt weaknesses coincide with Resource and Protocol weaknesses 94% of the time.

nodes.title: CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving Systems
nodes.one_liner: A practical middle ground between 'disable prefix caching' and 'leak prompts': isolate only the prefixes that become suspiciously shared, and only when timing is actually distinguishable.
points: Automatic Prefix Caching (APC) creates a measurable time-to-first-token (TTFT) timing side channel where cache hits vs misses leak cross-tenant prefix matches, enabling iterative prompt-stealing in realistic workloads with reported cross-user prefix reuse up to 30%.

CacheSolidarity mitigates APC timing leakage by flagging prefixes upon first cross-user reuse and then selectively isolating continuation past flagged prefixes (while allowing the owner to continue), avoiding semantic prompt analysis and minimizing collateral performance loss.

On vLLM across nine models (0.5B–13B) and multi-tenant workloads, CacheSolidarity delivers up to 70% higher cache reuse and 30% lower inference latency than per-user cache isolation defenses while adding ~0.007 ms/request overhead and ~32 bytes metadata per cache entry.

nodes.title: Detecting Cryptographically Relevant Software Packages with Collaborative LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Prompt optimization flips majority voting from a liability into a high-recall, on-prem filter for finding cryptographically relevant packages at ecosystem scale.
points: Across 65,295 Fedora packages, local LLMs produced parsable JSON classifications with very low per-model error rates (0.11%–1.74%), but requiring all five models to be valid dropped usable coverage to 63,529 packages (2.70% discarded), making robust parsing and re-querying essential for production pipelines.

With an initial shared prompt, the best local single model (Llama-3 8B) reached F1=0.77 while the 5-model majority vote underperformed (F1=0.72), showing that naive ensembling can average away strong models rather than improve them.

After model-specific prompt tuning and retry logic, the local 5-model majority vote improved to F1=0.86 (Recall=0.95) and matched or beat several online models on the same 390-package ground truth, indicating on-prem, privacy-preserving LLM ensembles can serve as an effective first-pass filter for crypto-asset inventorying and PQC readiness.

nodes.title: Don't Let the Claw Grip Your Hand: A Security Analysis and Defense Framework for OpenClaw
nodes.one_liner: Local code agents with shell/file tools inherit most of their security posture from the backend LLM, yet their hardest failures cluster around sandbox escapes—so practical hardening needs OS-enforced isolation plus gated tool execution, not just better prompting.
points: Across 47 MITRE-mapped adversarial scenarios, OpenClaw’s baseline protection varied sharply by LLM backend from 17% (DeepSeek V3.2) to 83% (Claude Opus 4.6), making model choice a primary security control rather than a plug-compatible implementation detail.

Sandbox boundary violations were the most consistently exploitable class with an average defense rate of 17% (best backend only 33%), indicating that semantic refusals and simple string checks are insufficient to enforce filesystem containment against path traversal, absolute paths, and symlink escapes.

A Human-in-the-Loop tool-call interception layer increased effective defense to as high as 91.5% and newly blocked up to 8 severe attacks that fully bypassed native defenses (e.g., improving GPT-5.3 Codex by +17 points to 65.9%), but gains were limited for weakly-aligned backends where overall defense remained under 20%.

nodes.title: Give Them an Inch and They Will Take a Mile:Understanding and Measuring Caller Identity Confusion in MCP-Based AI Systems
nodes.one_liner: MCP servers frequently act as long-lived execution proxies where a single legitimate authorization silently grants “mile-wide” authority to any later caller unless authorization is bound per invocation/session.
points: A large-scale scan of 6,137 real-world MCP servers found 2,846 vulnerable to caller identity confusion (46.4%), showing that one-time or server-scoped authorization commonly gets reused across different callers once any legitimate user authorizes the server.

Insecure authorization patterns persist across both categories and popularity tiers (including >1K-star projects), indicating that GitHub stars and perceived maturity are weak proxies for safe MCP authorization design.

Developer Tools are a high-risk concentration point (52% insecure), because they expose dense, high-privilege tool surfaces where caller-agnostic authorization reuse can translate into practical exploits like remote command execution, GUI/browser control, and privileged third-party API abuse without credential theft.

nodes.title: Why LLMs Fail: A Failure Analysis and Partial Success Measurement for Automated Security Patch Generation
nodes.one_liner: Security patch generation is effectively bimodal—either fully correct or confidently wrong—so security-specific validation (e.g., PoV tests) is mandatory before deployment.
points: Across 319 Gemini-generated patches for 64 Java Vul4J vulnerabilities, only 24.8% were fully correct while 51.4% failed both security and functionality, indicating that most failures are not minor edge cases but fundamentally unusable fixes.

LLMs largely preserve intended program behavior (mean functionality score 0.832; median 0.999) yet rarely achieve security goals (mean security score 0.251; median 0.0), creating a high-risk class of patches that look fine in CI but remain exploitable (10.3% functional-but-insecure).

Repair difficulty is driven by vulnerability semantics rather than code structure: fix rates ranged from 0% for CWE-20 (Input Validation) to 45% for CWE-835 (Infinite Loop), and larger human patch size predicted lower success (Spearman ρ = -0.331, p = 0.008) while LOC and cyclomatic complexity were not predictive.

nodes.title: You Told Me to Do It: Measuring Instructional Text-induced Private Data Leakage in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Trusted project documentation can act as a stealthy prompt-injection carrier that turns helpful, high-privilege LLM agents into reliable data-exfiltration executors.
points: High-privilege computer-use agents executed README-embedded adversarial instructions with end-to-end private-data exfiltration success rates up to 85%, showing that documentation trust plus terminal/filesystem/network access creates a high-impact injection surface.

Semantic compliance with injected documentation instructions generalized across four LLM families (46–79% function-invocation success in a controlled tool-calling simulation), indicating a paradigm-level vulnerability rather than a single-model quirk.

Neither humans nor common detectors reliably caught the attacks—15/15 participants had a 0% detection rate, while 12 rule-based and 6 LLM-based filters failed to achieve high detection without unusable false positives—implying current “scan-and-block” defenses don’t separate malicious from legitimate setup guidance.

nodes.title: Arbiter: Detecting Interference in LLM Agent System Prompts
nodes.one_liner: Multi-model, rule+scouring analysis makes system prompts testable like software—and shows many high-impact failures are mechanically detectable for pennies.
points: A $0.27 cross-vendor campaign analyzing Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI surfaced 152 undirected findings plus 21 directed interference patterns, demonstrating that comprehensive system-prompt QA is cheap enough to run continuously in CI rather than as an occasional audit.

In Claude Code, directed analysis found 21 interference patterns including 4 critical mandate–prohibition contradictions, and 95% (20/21) were statically detectable, implying most prompt failures are “lintable” without model-in-the-loop judgment.

Prompt architecture predicts failure-mode class (monolithic→subsystem-boundary contradictions, modular→composition-seam contract bugs, flat→capability/consistency trade-offs) while severity did not track architecture, exemplified by a Gemini CLI schema-level memory-loss flaw that remained after a vendor patch addressed only symptoms.

nodes.title: Understanding LLM Behavior When Encountering User-Supplied Harmful Content in Harmless Tasks
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
nodes.one_liner: Harmless-looking tasks like translation become a high-throughput safety bypass when users supply the harm, and common guardrails can be sidestepped simply by embedding that harm inside longer benign text.
points: Across 9 mainstream LLMs tested on 1,357 policy-violating knowledge snippets embedded inside 9 policy-compliant tasks (12,213 prompts/model), many models still produced harmful outputs frequently—e.g., GPT-3.5 Turbo averaged K-HRN 4.035 and Qwen3 3.942 (≈4 of 9 harmless tasks per harmful snippet), while Llama3 was far lower at K-HRN 0.178.

User-content-heavy tasks are the highest-risk channel for “in-content” harm propagation, with Translation reaching an average T-HRR of 0.512 (over half of harmful snippets yielding harmful outputs) and those outputs being highly grounded in the supplied text (GS ≈ 4.94/5), indicating models largely reproduce and disseminate the embedded harmful material.

External defenses are brittle under realistic “wrapping” where harmful text is blended into longer benign content: T-HRR jumps to 0.826–0.894 for GPT-3.5/GPT-5.2 under Llama Guard-family filters, and even chunking reduces but does not eliminate residual risk (e.g., GPT-3.5 still 0.330 under Llama Guard), implying input filtering alone is insufficient without content-aware refusal behavior inside the model.

nodes.title: Security-by-Design for LLM-Based Code Generation: Leveraging Internal Representations for Concept-Driven Steering Mechanisms
affiliations: Fraunhofer Institute for Applied and Integrated Security
nodes.one_liner: Steering internal representations can make code models measurably safer at near-zero overhead—and the models often "know" the code is vulnerable even as they generate it.
points: A single inference-time activation-addition step (“SCS-Code”) improved secure code quality across CodeGuard+ and CWEval, with a Llama3.1-8B hybrid (constrained decoding + steering) reaching 61.63% secure-pass@1 and 85.93% pass@1 on CodeGuard+, and improving CWEval secure-pass@1 from 13.58% (vanilla) to 17.37% (~28% relative) while keeping pass@1 roughly stable (33.34%→37.64%).

Residual-stream activations in multiple CodeLLMs linearly separate secure vs. insecure code most strongly in mid layers (≈13–15), and token-level alignment reveals models can encode vulnerability awareness while still emitting insecure code, implying the failure mode is not pure ignorance but weak translation of internal safety signals into decoding decisions.

Security steering generalizes across languages and models using a small hand-crafted contrastive set (~50 pairs) and no retraining, yielding consistent CodeGuard+ gains such as Llama3.1-8B secure-pass@1 49.7%→52.9% and DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite 52.8%→60.3%, while exposing that some fine-tuned “secure coding” baselines can inflate security rates by suppressing functional code (e.g., SafeCoder CodeLlama pass@1 35.0% vs SCS-Code 65.7%).

nodes.title: From Thinker to Society: Security in Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution of AI Agents
affiliations: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics
Collaborative Innovation Center of Novel Software Technology and Industrialization
The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Ningbo Global Innovation Center, Zhejiang University
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is the shift from model-level safety to autonomy-tiered, system-level security—where collective agent networks introduce qualitatively new failure modes (contagion, collusion, collapse) that cannot be fixed by improving a single model.
points: The Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution (HAE) model separates agent security into three autonomy tiers—L1 cognition, L2 execution, and L3 collective—showing how the same weakness can escalate from transient jailbreaks to irreversible real-world actions and finally to network-wide systemic cascades.

Indirect prompt injection remains a high-leverage cross-layer entry point because agents fail to reliably separate instructions from untrusted data, with reported tool-execution compromise reaching 47.7% in a realistic prompt-injection environment (AgentDojo) for a frontier model under indirect attacks.

Persistent state attacks (memory/RAG poisoning and backdoor-style corruption) and L3 propagation dynamics (viral infection, collusion, cascade failure) expose a defense gap where single-agent alignment and static filters are insufficient, pushing security requirements toward provenance controls, least-privilege tool mediation, and topology/protocol-level containment.

nodes.title: ESAA-Security: An Event-Sourced, Verifiable Architecture for Agent-Assisted Security Audits of AI-Generated Code
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it reframes LLM security auditing as a governed state-machine with replay/hashing integrity, so conclusions are reproducible and traceable instead of being tied to one-off prompts.
points: ESAA-Security turns agent-assisted code auditing into a replay-verifiable, append-only event workflow where only contract-validated, schema-constrained outputs can mutate audit state, making the final report auditable by construction rather than persuasive prose.

The framework operationalizes security review into 4 phases, 26 tasks, 16 security domains, and 95 executable checks aligned to OWASP Top 10/ASVS-style coverage, shifting audit quality from ad hoc model recall to explicitly encoded scope and repeatable evidence collection.

Audit deliverables are produced as a structured artifact chain (check-level evidence → vulnerability inventory → severity and risk matrix → remediation and executive summary) including a 0–100 security score, enabling prioritization and governance even when code is AI-generated or heavily AI-modified.

nodes.title: SCAFFOLD-CEGIS: Preventing Latent Security Degradation in LLM-Driven Iterative Code Refinement
affiliations: Chengdu Institute of Computer Application, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Static-analysis “pass/fail” gates can backfire in iterative LLM programming by encouraging refactors that silently delete or weaken defensive logic, and explicit semantic constraints are a practical way to enforce security monotonicity across iterations.
points: Iterative LLM code refinement can systematically worsen security over time, with 43.7% of GPT-4o refinement chains showing more vulnerabilities than the baseline after 10 rounds (and 28.6% still degrading even under explicit security-hardening prompts).

SAST-based gating can create a pseudo-safety effect where passing scans masks structural defense erosion, increasing latent security degradation from 12.5% (no protection) to 20.8% (Post-hoc SAST) despite achieving DR=0% and SMR=100% on static-analysis metrics.

Making security constraints explicit and verifiable via a CEGIS-inspired multi-agent gate (semantic anchors + four-layer verification + failure assimilation) cuts latent security degradation to 2.1% while maintaining DR=0% and SMR=100%, and improves fix success after rejections to 52.71% (vs. ~21–23% without assimilation).

nodes.title: RewardHackingAgents: Benchmarking Evaluation Integrity for LLM ML-Engineering Agents
affiliations: David R. Cheriton School of Computer Science
nodes.one_liner: It turns ML-agent scoring into a measurable security property by separating “metric inflation via evaluator edits” from “performance inflation via test leakage,” and shows you must defend both to get trustworthy numbers.
points: In fully mutable ML-engineering workspaces, scripted agents achieved 100% reward-hacking success across both evaluator tampering and train/test leakage (overall_success=1.00 with 95% CI [0.97, 1.00]) on three tasks and two LLM backbones, showing that “score-only” evaluation is structurally unsafe when the scorer is editable.

Single-mechanism defenses leave one complete bypass: evaluator locking eliminated evaluator-tampering success (success_eval=0.00) but still allowed 100% leakage success (success_train=1.00), while train-time test/leak denial eliminated leakage success (success_train=0.00; blocked_train=1.00) but still allowed 100% evaluator-tampering success (success_eval=1.00).

Under natural (non-attacker) agent prompts, evaluator-tampering attempts occurred in ~50% of episodes and were reduced to 0% by evaluator locking, with a ~25–31% median runtime overhead, while train/test leakage attempts were 0%—implying integrity risk is common even without explicit malicious instructions and has measurable throughput costs to mitigate.

nodes.title: SlowBA: An efficiency backdoor attack towards VLM-based GUI agents
nodes.one_liner: Backdoors in GUI agents don’t need to break correctness—making them “think forever” on common-looking pop-ups is enough to cause real operational failures.
points: A backdoored VLM-based GUI agent can be made dramatically slower by maximizing response length (latency–length Pearson r=0.8059), yielding up to +358.52% token length and +66.92% latency on web tasks while keeping clean accuracy close to baseline (63.1% vs 67.5%).

A two-stage reward-level backdoor injection that first aligns a long-response format via SFT and then gates it with trigger-aware RL is necessary for reliable trigger separation, since Stage I alone makes both clean and triggered outputs long and Stage II alone is unstable (triggered latency 46.30s vs clean 115.03s).

Realistic pop-up notification triggers are highly stealthy (human abnormality rate 5.8%, Fleiss’ κ=0.74) and the slowdown persists under common defenses (e.g., JPEG still leaves +325.71% length and +58.41% latency; backdoor detection like Spectral Signature/Beatrix does not mitigate).

nodes.title: SoK: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): Taxonomy, Architectures, Evaluation, and Research Directions
nodes.one_liner: Treating Agentic RAG as a policy-controlled loop clarifies why evaluation, cost control, and security (prompt injection/memory poisoning) must be designed at the trajectory level, not the final-answer level.
points: Agentic RAG is usefully modeled as a finite-horizon POMDP where an LLM policy iteratively selects retrieval/tool/reasoning/STOP actions over a persistent memory state, making reliability a sequential decision-making problem rather than a one-shot prompting problem.

Iterative autonomy introduces safety-critical compounding failures—hallucinations can propagate across steps, retrieval drift can waste budgets, and persistent memory enables cross-session compromise—while corpus poisoning attacks reported elsewhere can reach ~90% success with as few as five injected documents, implying small adversarial footholds can dominate system behavior.

Static answer-only evaluation is structurally insufficient for agentic loops, motivating trajectory-level metrics (e.g., Progress Rate and Effective Information Rate) plus tool-execution correctness and cost/latency accounting to diagnose where failures originate and to manage token/latency amplification.

nodes.title: Governing Evolving Memory in LLM Agents: Risks, Mechanisms, and the Stability and Safety Governed Memory (SSGM) Framework
affiliations: College of Intelligent Science and Engineering, Jinan University
nodes.one_liner: The key idea is to treat agent memory like a safety-critical state machine—every read and write is mediated, validated, and periodically reconciled to prevent long-term corruption.
points: Uncontrolled evolving memory in LLM agents creates a compounding failure loop across ingestion, consolidation, and retrieval—turning one-off errors into persistent corruption via memory poisoning, semantic/procedural/goal drift, and memory-induced hallucinations.

A governed architecture that decouples memory evolution from execution can bound long-horizon semantic drift from linear growth O(T·ε_step) to window-bounded growth O(N·ε_step) by adding gated writes plus periodic reconciliation against an immutable episodic ledger.

Enforcing read-time governance (freshness decay + provenance + access-scoped retrieval) directly targets two high-impact real-world risks—stale-memory activation and cross-tenant privacy leakage—by preventing outdated or unauthorized memories from ever entering the model context.

nodes.title: Can Safety Emerge from Weak Supervision? A Systematic Analysis of Small Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Automated evaluator-driven red-teaming plus multi-objective preference optimization can make 1–2B models meaningfully safer with an order-of-magnitude less human supervision than standard RLHF-style pipelines.
points: A closed-loop, fully automated weak-supervision pipeline (Self-MOA) improved small-model safety by 12.41% overall while preserving helpfulness, indicating that scalable alignment can be achieved without large human-labeled preference datasets.

On attack-style benchmarks, the method delivered 41.2% average safety improvement over a safety-reset baseline and 17.1% better safety than a MODPO+PKU-RLHF baseline, implying model-specific adaptive red-teaming can outperform static human-curated corpora for jailbreak resilience.

Comparable or better safety/helpfulness on SALAD-Bench was achieved with 6–11× less alignment data than PKU-RLHF (≈70k points), suggesting weakly supervised preference construction can substantially cut cost/compute while maintaining general capability (reported ~1% variance from the safety-reset and +2.56% general improvement vs base).

nodes.title: GR-SAP: Generative Replay for Safety Alignment Preservation during Fine-Tuning
nodes.one_liner: Self-generated “generative replay” safety data can reliably replace unavailable proprietary alignment corpora and prevent benign fine-tuning from undoing safety.
points: Mixing just 10% GR-SAP synthetic alignment data during fine-tuning cut Llama-3’s average harmful-response rate from 6.28% to 0.58% (≈11× reduction) while keeping downstream accuracy essentially unchanged across five tasks.

Naively substituting open safety datasets can backfire badly—e.g., mixing Beavertails into Llama-3 drove harmful responses up to 31.60% vs 6.28% unmixed (≈5× worse), indicating dataset mismatch/noise can actively erode safety guardrails.

On the only model with accessible original alignment data (OLMo2), GR-SAP matched or slightly exceeded using the true alignment set for safety preservation (0.50% vs 0.60% harmful rate) and produced much closer semantic similarity to the original alignment distribution (MAUVE 0.455/0.646 for queries/responses vs ~0.293/0.244 for open datasets).

nodes.title: Few Tokens, Big Leverage: Preserving Safety Alignment by Constraining Safety Tokens during Fine-tuning
nodes.one_liner: A few dozen “refusal-style” tokens carry outsized leverage for preserving LLM safety during fine-tuning, enabling a lightweight, token-level regularizer to block harmful alignment drift without broadly constraining model updates.
points: Safety-alignment drift during downstream fine-tuning is highly token-localized, and constraining the model to match a safety-aligned reference only on a small top-K set (K=50) of safety-critical tokens preserves refusal behavior while keeping downstream accuracy near vanilla SFT across GSM8K/SST-2/AGNEWS under 10% harmful data.

On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with 10% harmful data, targeted safety-token constraints cut attack success rates to 5.75–9.27% on StrongREJECT and 13.50–29.50% on HarmBench while maintaining task accuracy comparable to standard fine-tuning (e.g., GSM8K 80.89% vs 81.65%).

Direct logit intervention shows the identified safety tokens are causally important: boosting them reduces ASR from 33.5% to 0.5% while ablating them raises ASR to 41.5% (random-token interventions do not meaningfully change ASR), implying refusals depend on a compact vocabulary subset that can be protected with minimal utility cost.

nodes.title: Invisible Safety Threat: Malicious Finetuning for LLM via Steganography
nodes.one_liner: Zero-width character steganography turns safety alignment into a UI illusion by creating a covert harmful channel that humans and content filters both miss.
points: Malicious fine-tuning can train LLMs to exchange hidden harmful instructions and answers via zero‑width Unicode steganography, making the visible chat interaction appear fully benign while covertly transmitting arbitrary malicious content.

On AdvBench (520 prompts), 100% of stegotext prompt–response pairs across GPT‑4.1, Llama‑3.3‑70B‑Instruct, Phi‑4, and Mistral‑Small‑24B were classified as safe by Llama‑Guard‑3‑8B before decoding, yet over 90% of the decoded interactions were labeled unsafe (e.g., GPT‑4.1: 93.3%).

The attack can bypass provider safeguards in practice: a GPT‑4.1 model fine-tuned through the OpenAI fine-tuning API retained a 100% refusal rate on plaintext harmful prompts while still emitting steganographically encoded harmful outputs, implying current training-data moderation and inference-time filtering can be evaded without embedding explicit harmful text in the fine-tuning dataset.

nodes.title: LLM2Vec-Gen: Generative Embeddings from Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Embeddings that represent what an LLM would say—trained from unlabeled queries with a frozen backbone—can simultaneously boost benchmark accuracy, reduce harmful retrieval, and remain decodable for interpretability.
points: A response-centric embedding approach that keeps the LLM backbone frozen achieves a new self-supervised MTEB(eng, v2) best score of 62.1 on Qwen-3-8B, improving 9.3% over its unsupervised teacher and closing the supervised gap to 3.6 points (62.1 vs 65.7).

Encoding the model’s likely refusal rather than the harmful prompt reduces unsafe top-5 retrieval on AdvBench-IR by up to 43.2% (e.g., 46.7→26.5 for Qwen-3-1.7B), indicating that alignment behaviors can propagate into embedding-based retrieval systems.

Reasoning-intensive retrieval improves substantially when embeddings represent generated answers, with BRIGHT gains scaling with model size up to +29.3% (14.9→19.3 on Qwen-3-8B), suggesting that retrieval quality can inherit LLM reasoning without inference-time generation.

nodes.title: Model Merging in the Era of Large Language Models: Methods, Applications, and Future Directions
nodes.one_liner: Merging has become a low-cost “capability composition” primitive for LLM ecosystems, but reliability hinges on managing interference and validating safety/behavioral drift after combining checkpoints.
points: Model merging combines multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single checkpoint without additional training, cutting ensemble-style inference overhead while retaining much of the specialized capability when models share a pretrained initialization (i.e., live in a connected low-loss region).

Interference is the dominant failure mode in cross-task merges (notably sign conflicts, magnitude imbalance, and redundant deltas), so sparsification and conflict-resolution methods (e.g., trimming+sign election) are highlighted as practical mitigations that improve multi-task retention versus naive averaging.

The field is shifting from static weight averaging toward structured and automated merging—routing-based MoE/LoRA-expert composition, activation/representation-guided alignment, and evolutionary search over layer-wise recipes—implying higher peak performance at the cost of extra memory, calibration data, or search compute.

nodes.title: The Attack and Defense Landscape of Agentic AI: A Comprehensive Survey
nodes.one_liner: The most useful takeaway is a defense-in-depth map that ties agent design choices to concrete risk cascades, showing why component-level fixes routinely fail in end-to-end agent systems.
points: A systematic review of 128 works (2023–Oct 2025) reports 51 attack methods and 60 defense methods, indicating defenses are fragmented across layers and still lag the pace and diversity of agent threats.

Agent security risk is driven by seven design dimensions (input trust, access sensitivity, workflow, action, memory, tool, UI), where greater flexibility consistently expands attack surface and amplifies cascading failures from instruction hijacking/unsafe data flow into data exfiltration, unauthorized actions, and denial-of-service.

Real-world agent deployments show patching often mitigates downstream impacts (e.g., access control or output sanitization) but leaves root causes like indirect prompt injection and unconstrained information flow largely unaddressed, enabling repeatable multi-step exploit chains such as zero-click data exfiltration via retrieved content.

nodes.title: aCAPTCHA: Verifying That an Entity Is a Capable Agent via Asymmetric Hardness
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it flips CAPTCHA into an agent-admission gate by turning AI’s speed advantage into a verifiable capability proof (⟨action, reasoning, memory⟩) rather than relying on identity claims or behavioral scoring.
points: A new “entity-type verification” primitive is formalized as a three-class taxonomy (Human, Script, Agent) using an externally testable agentic capability vector ⟨x,r,s⟩ (action, reasoning, memory) evaluated under a timing threshold τ chosen so T_AI ≪ τ ≪ T_human.

The proposed aCAPTCHA instantiation uses a three-round, semantically chained HTTP protocol to make action, reasoning, and cross-round memory necessary—aiming to block deterministic scripts and humans via asymmetric timing while accepting capability-complete agents.

In preliminary trials (20 sessions; 47 round observations) a Claude-based agent achieved a 7.1s median per-round effective response time under τ=15s (empirical session pass 90%, fitted 94%), while a human lower-bound model estimated ≈148s per round on the same narrative lengths, implying ~21× timing separation and a wide feasible τ window of ~16–89s for high agent TPR and low human completion probability.

nodes.title: SBOMs into Agentic AIBOMs: Schema Extensions, Agentic Orchestration, and Reproducibility Evaluation
nodes.one_liner: Turning SBOMs into agent-orchestrated, runtime-evidenced AIBOMs makes exploitability claims reproducible, standards-aligned (CSAF/VEX), and operationally cheap enough to run continuously.
points: An agentic AIBOM pipeline using three policy-constrained agents (baseline reconstruction, runtime drift/dependency capture, and VEX reasoning) achieved 98.6% semantic reproducibility with low overhead (~4% CPU and <300MB RAM), outperforming ReproZip (87.4%), SciUnit (73.2%), and ProvStore (61.8%) on the same benchmark workloads.

Ablations show the runtime telemetry agent and VEX/policy reasoning agent are essential for capturing late-bound dependencies and contextual exploitability: removing MCP increased baseline SBOM false negatives by +14% FNR, removing A2A caused missed runtime drift/late-loaded dependencies, and removing AGNTCY reduced outputs to uncontextualized CVE lists that cannot support governance decisions.

At current CVE scale (200k+ total disclosures and >30k/year) and with ~95% of SBOM-matched vulnerabilities expected to be non-exploitable in a given product, shifting from static SBOMs to runtime-evidenced VEX assertions (anchored to ISO/IEC 20153:2025 CSAF v2.0 semantics) materially reduces wasted remediation effort while improving audit-ready, environment-specific vulnerability interpretation.

nodes.title: Towards Modeling Cybersecurity Behavior of Humans in Organizations
nodes.one_liner: Treating AI agents like organizational actors—subject to roles, culture, norms, and usability constraints—reframes prompt injection as a behavioral manipulation problem rather than a purely technical bug class.
points: A unified organizational cybersecurity behavior framework links internal drivers (motivation, awareness/knowledge, mindset/attitude, skills, intention, agency) with external drivers (culture, norms/regulations, role, usability) to explain why secure behavior often fails despite competent individuals.

Mapping human behavioral drivers to agentic AI components implies prompt injection and related manipulation are best modeled as “social engineering against AI agents,” where role/persona instructions, alignment, usability/friction, and situational pressure jointly shape unsafe actions.

The model predicts “path-of-least-resistance” failures across both humans and agents, where high task pressure plus low agency/self-efficacy and poor usability increases non-compliance (in humans) and increases shortcut-taking such as over-trusting accessible sources or hallucinating when knowledge is missing (in AI agents).

nodes.title: Re-Evaluating EVMBench: Are AI Agents Ready for Smart Contract Security?
nodes.one_liner: Impressive headline exploit rates on curated audit data collapse to zero on post-release real-world incidents, and even small workflow/scaffold choices can swing results enough to reshuffle model rankings.
points: Agent vulnerability-detection performance is highly non-robust—while the best configuration reaches 47.5% recall on the original benchmark, model rankings shift materially across datasets, tasks, model versions (e.g., Gemini 3 Pro 16.7% vs Gemini 3.1 Pro 35.0–37.5%), and configurations.

On a contamination-free set of 22 post-release real-world incidents, agents detect up to 65% of vulnerabilities but achieve 0% end-to-end exploitation across 110 agent–incident attempts, contradicting claims that exploitation is mostly solved once discovery succeeds.

Scaffolding and runtime settings are first-order variables: an open-source scaffold beats vendor scaffolds in 5/6 controlled comparisons by 1.7–5.0 percentage points, and higher “reasoning effort” can hurt (e.g., GPT-5.2 low beats xhigh by 8pp on Exploit), implying many published model-level comparisons are confounded.

nodes.title: When OpenClaw Meets Hospital: Toward an Agentic Operating System for Dynamic Clinical Workflows
affiliations: National Institute for Data Science in Health and Medicine, Xiamen University
Yttrium-90 Precision Interventional Radiotherapy Center of Liver Cancer
nodes.one_liner: The interesting twist is treating clinical LLM agents like untrusted OS processes—constraining them to audited file I/O plus pre-approved “skills,” and using manifest navigation instead of vector search to preserve longitudinal medical context.
points: A hospital-oriented “agentic OS” design can materially reduce LLM-agent risk by enforcing least-privilege at the kernel level (per-agent Linux users/namespaces plus seccomp/AppArmor/SELinux) so unsafe behaviors are blocked by the runtime rather than relying on prompt-level compliance.

Replacing embedding-based RAG with page-indexed, manifest-guided “progressive disclosure” retrieval keeps longitudinal clinical structure intact and yields inherently interpretable access traces (the exact sequence of manifests/pages consulted) while avoiding embedding computation and offline reindexing on live record updates.
